title: Costs, scale of production and break-even analysis
summary: 7115 O-Level — break-even formula, CVP chart, margin of safety, target output, and evaluation of assumptions.
learningObjectives: Calculate break-even output using contribution per unit.
Draw and interpret break-even charts (TR, TC, FC).
Calculate margin of safety in units and as a percentage.
Calculate output required for a target profit.
Evaluate assumptions and limitations of break-even analysis.

simpleExplanation.title: Where profit starts
simpleExplanation.summary: Break-even is the output level where total revenue equals total cost — no profit, no loss. Each unit sold contributes toward fixed costs; once enough contribution is earned, the business covers all costs.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of fixed costs as the entry fee for a theme park. Every ticket sold (contribution per unit) chips away at that fee. Break-even is the number of tickets you must sell before any money counts as profit — like filling the park to the point where the entry fees are fully paid off.
steps: Contribution per unit = selling price − variable cost per unit.
Break-even output (units) = fixed costs ÷ contribution per unit.
On a chart, the total revenue and total cost lines cross at break-even.
Margin of safety = actual (or budgeted) output − break-even output.
State assumptions (constant price, linear costs) when evaluating.

flashcards.back: The level of output at which total revenue equals total cost — zero profit and zero loss.
Break-even output = fixed costs ÷ contribution per unit.
Selling price minus variable cost per unit — the amount each unit contributes toward fixed costs and profit.
Actual or budgeted output minus break-even output — how far sales can fall before the business makes a loss.

flashcards.front: Margin of safety as a percentage?

flashcards.back: (Fixed costs + target profit) ÷ contribution per unit.

flashcards.front: What does the break-even chart show?
flashcards.back: Fixed costs (horizontal), total cost (FC + variable costs), and total revenue — intersection of TR and TC is break-even.
Selling price and variable cost per unit are constant; costs behave linearly with output.

flashcards.front: Why is a higher margin of safety desirable?
flashcards.back: It means the business can absorb a fall in sales before hitting losses — lower risk.

flashcards.front: Limitation of break-even for multi-product firms?
flashcards.back: Assumes a single product or constant sales mix — unrealistic when product mix changes.

faq.q: Why use contribution costing for break-even, not full costing?
faq.a: Break-even is a short-run decision tool. Only variable costs change with output; fixed costs are covered by total contribution. Full (absorption) costing spreads overheads into unit cost and is not appropriate for break-even calculations.

faq.q: Can break-even be expressed in revenue (£) as well as units?
faq.a: Yes. Break-even revenue = fixed costs ÷ contribution-to-sales ratio (contribution per unit ÷ selling price). Examiners often ask for units; know both forms.

faq.q: What if contribution per unit is zero or negative?
faq.a: If SP ≤ VC, each unit sold loses money on variable costs alone — break-even is impossible at that price. The business must raise price or cut variable costs before break-even analysis is meaningful.

sections.content: Break-even analysis (5.4.4) is one of the most examined calculations in **7115 AS** finance. Examiners expect you to **state the formula**, **show working**, **interpret a chart**, and **evaluate assumptions** — often in the same question. Link it back to **contribution costing** (5.4.2): break-even only works when you correctly separate fixed and variable costs.

Before calculating break-even, find **contribution per unit**:

**Contribution per unit = selling price − variable cost per unit**

Total contribution from all units sold must cover **fixed costs** before any profit is made. At break-even, total contribution exactly equals fixed costs.

Break-even output (units) = $\frac{\text{Fixed costs}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$

Break-even revenue = $\frac{\text{Fixed costs}}{\text{Contribution per unit} \div \text{Selling price}}$ (or FC ÷ C/S ratio)

items: **Common error:** Using full (absorption) unit cost instead of variable cost — always deduct only variable costs from selling price.
**Units matter:** Examiners often give fixed costs in £000 and VC in £ per unit — convert consistently before dividing.
**Zero contribution:** If contribution per unit is zero, break-even is undefined — the price only covers variable costs.

sections.content:
A break-even chart plots **cost and revenue against output** (units on the x-axis, £ on the y-axis):

1. **Fixed costs** — a horizontal line (costs that do not change with output).
2. **Total cost** — starts at fixed costs and slopes upward (FC + total variable costs).
3. **Total revenue** — starts at the origin and slopes upward (price × quantity).

The **break-even point** is where TR and TC intersect. The **profit region** is to the right of break-even (TR > TC); the **loss region** is to the left.

When drawing or interpreting a chart in an exam, label all three lines (FC, TC, TR), mark the break-even point, and shade or annotate the profit and loss regions. Examiners award marks for clear, correctly labelled diagrams.

The **margin of safety** measures how far actual (or budgeted) sales can fall before the business starts making a loss. A **larger** margin of safety means **lower risk** — the firm has a buffer against falling demand.

Margin of safety (units) = Actual output − Break-even output

Margin of safety (%) = $\frac{\text{Actual output} − \text{Break-even output}}{\text{Actual output}} \times 100$
Managers often need output for a **target profit**, not just break-even. Total contribution must cover fixed costs **and** the required profit.
Output for target profit = $\frac{\text{Fixed costs} + \text{Target profit}}{\text{Contribution per unit}}$

sections.question:
Nova Crafts sells handmade lamps for $48 each. Variable cost is $18 per lamp and fixed costs are $12 000 per month.

(a) Calculate the break-even output in units.
(b) The business currently sells 450 lamps per month. Calculate the margin of safety in units and as a percentage.
sections.solution:
(a) **Contribution per unit**
Contribution = $48 − $18 = **$30** per lamp

**Break-even output**
BE = $12 000 ÷ $30 = **400 units** per month

(b) **Margin of safety (units)**
MoS = 450 − 400 = **50 units**

**Margin of safety (%)**
MoS% = (50 ÷ 450) × 100 = **11.1%** (to 1 d.p.)

The business can afford a fall of 50 units (11.1%) before reaching break-even.

sections.question:
Using the same data (SP $48, VC $18, FC $12 000, contribution $30 per unit):

Calculate the output required to achieve a monthly profit of $3 000.
sections.solution:
**Output for target profit**
Required contribution = Fixed costs + Target profit
= $12 000 + $3 000 = $15 000

Output = $15 000 ÷ $30 = **500 units** per month

Check: at 500 units, contribution = 500 × $30 = $15 000. After fixed costs ($12 000), profit = $3 000 ✓

sections.content: Break-even analysis is useful for **short-run decisions** (pricing, launch, capacity) but relies on simplifying assumptions. In evaluation questions, name the assumption and explain why it may not hold in reality.

items: **Constant selling price** — discounts, competition, or bulk pricing may change the price per unit.
**Constant variable cost per unit** — bulk buying or overtime may change VC as output rises.
**Fixed costs stay fixed** — step costs (e.g. new machinery) can jump at higher output.
**Single product or constant mix** — multi-product firms need weighted average contribution.
**Linear relationships** — real cost/revenue curves may not be straight lines.

sections.content: In **evaluate** or **discuss** questions, do not only list limitations — link each to the business context in the case study. For example: "A single selling price is assumed, but the case mentions online discounts, so actual break-even may be higher than calculated."

interactiveEmbed.title: Break-even analysis
interactiveEmbed.hint: Adjust fixed costs, variable cost per unit, and price — watch TR and TC cross at break-even (9609 5.4.4).

steps.caption: Break-even output = fixed costs ÷ contribution per unit.
steps.embedHint: Set FC and unit variable cost in the sim — read contribution.

steps.caption: On the chart, total revenue and total cost lines cross at break-even.
steps.embedHint: Watch the TR/TC intersection as you change price.

steps.caption: Margin of safety = actual output − break-even output.
steps.embedHint: Drag quantity past break-even — read the profit region.

steps.caption: Assumes constant price and linear variable costs.
steps.embedHint: Test limits — change price and see how BE shifts.
